summary: The author tackled an RSNA aneurysm detection competition in a rapid 8-day speedrun, initially attempting a segmentation model that plateaued at a low Dice score. Abandoning segmentation, they implemented a DINOv3 ViT to predict ROI crop coordinates from equidistant CT slices, preserving over 95% of aneurysm locations. The core pipeline relied on a CoaT-Lite-Medium classifier trained on 384x384 ROI crops, leveraging rotation, 2.5D slice combinations, and soft pseudo-distillation on negative samples to overcome extreme class imbalance. Final patient-level predictions were generated by max-aggregating per-image outputs, with an ensemble of CoaT-Lite-Medium and MaxViT securing a 4th place finish.
extensive_summary:
## Initial Approach & ROI Localization
The author approached the competition as a speedrun with only 14 days remaining, ultimately finishing in 8 days. They initially experimented with a segmentation model but abandoned it after it failed to learn beyond a ~0.6 Dice score in the limited timeframe. Instead, they shifted to a localization strategy using a DINOv3 ViT that processes equidistant 48 slices (input shape: 48x1x128x128) to predict bounding box coordinates (x1, x2, y1, y2) for ROI cropping. The author intentionally kept the ROI relatively large to avoid missing aneurysms, validating that this configuration retained 95%+ of aneurysm locations within the cropped region.

## Classification Pipeline & Improvements
The classification stage utilized a highly imbalanced dataset of ~545k samples (roughly 1 positive in 250 to 1700 depending on label distribution). The baseline model was CoaT-Lite-Medium, trained on 384x384 ROI crops with a global batch size of 192 and AdamW optimizer (LR 1e-4 cosine annealing to ~3e-5). Initial augmentations included HFlip and VFlip. To push performance, the author incrementally added Rotate (-25, 25) and 2.5D slice combinations (-2, 0, +2). A major breakthrough came from applying soft pseudo-distillation on the remaining ~500k negative samples, which significantly improved the CV score from 0.805 to 0.89.

## Inference & Ensembling
During inference, the model predicts on every cropped image per patient, and final predictions are simply max-aggregated across all images for that patient. The author explicitly noted that weighted sampling did not help despite the severe class imbalance, and they opted out of TTA. The final submission was an ensemble of the improved CoaT-Lite-Medium and a MaxViT model, which pushed the private leaderboard score to 0.84 and secured a gold medal/4th place finish.
competition_flow: Raw CT slices are processed through a DINOv3 ViT to predict ROI coordinates, which are used to extract 384x384 crops fed into a CoaT-Lite-Medium/MaxViT classifier, with final patient-level predictions obtained via max-aggregation of per-image outputs.
ensembling: Ensembled CoaT-Lite-Medium and MaxViT models, with final patient-level predictions obtained by max-aggregating per-image outputs without TTA.
insights: Extreme class imbalance required a tailored pipeline that avoided weighted sampling to achieve stable learning.
Keeping the ROI crop relatively large preserved over 95% of aneurysm locations while maintaining computational efficiency.
Soft pseudo-distillation on the majority of negative samples was a critical driver for score improvement.
critical_findings: Weighted sampling failed to improve performance despite severe class imbalance.
Initial segmentation approach was inefficient and plateaued at a low Dice score (~0.6) under tight time constraints.
what_did_not_work: Training a segmentation model initially, which plateaued at ~0.6 Dice.
Weighted sampling for the highly imbalanced classification task.
